title: WARM: On the Benefits of Weight Averaged Reward Models
problem_statement: Aligning large language models with human preferences via reinforcement learning can lead to reward hacking, where models exploit reward model failures. This is driven by distribution shifts during RL and inconsistencies in human preferences. The core technical problem is designing reward models that remain reliable and robust under these conditions.

explicit_limitations.name: Reward hacking from distribution shifts
explicit_limitations.description: The RL process induces distribution shifts that can cause reward models to become unreliable, leading to exploitation.
evidence_phrases: distribution shifts during the RL process

evidence_refs.quote: distribution shifts during the RL process

explicit_limitations.name: Reward hacking from preference inconsistencies
explicit_limitations.description: Human preferences can be inconsistent, making reward models vulnerable to being gamed.
evidence_phrases: inconsistencies in human preferences

evidence_refs.quote: inconsistencies in human preferences

core_ideas.name: Weight Averaged Reward Models (WARM)
core_ideas.mechanism: Fine-tune multiple reward models and average their weights in weight space, leveraging linear mode connectivity from shared pre-training.
evidence_phrases: averaging them in the weight space
fine-tuned weights remain linearly mode connected when sharing the same pre-training

evidence_refs.quote: averaging them in the weight space

experiment_signals.setting: Summarization tasks using best-of-N and RL methods
experiment_signals.claim: WARM improves overall quality and alignment of LLM predictions, with a policy RL fine-tuned with WARM achieving a 79.4% win rate against a policy RL fine-tuned with a single RM.
evidence_phrases: a policy RL fine-tuned with WARM has a 79.4% win rate against a policy RL fine-tuned with a single RM

evidence_refs.quote: a policy RL fine-tuned with WARM has a 79.4% win rate against a policy RL fine-tuned with a single RM

title: Self-Generated Critiques Boost Reward Modeling for Language Models
problem_statement: Current reward models for aligning large language models with human preferences primarily produce scalar scores and struggle to incorporate natural language critiques. This limits their ability to fully capture and utilize nuanced feedback during training.

explicit_limitations.name: Scalar-only reward modeling
explicit_limitations.description: Existing reward models mainly output scalar scores and do not naturally incorporate language critiques, which may limit their alignment quality.

evidence_refs.quote: current reward models mainly produce scalar scores and struggle to incorporate critiques in a natural language format

core_ideas.name: Critic-RM framework
core_ideas.mechanism: A two-stage process that generates and filters high-quality self-generated critiques, then jointly fine-tunes the model on reward prediction and critique generation without extra supervision.
evidence_phrases: Critic-RM, a framework that improves reward models using self-generated critiques without extra supervision

evidence_refs.quote: Critic-RM, a framework that improves reward models using self-generated critiques without extra supervision

experiment_signals.setting: Reward modeling benchmarks
experiment_signals.claim: Critic-RM improves reward modeling accuracy by 3.7%-7.3% over standard reward models and LLM judges, and improves reasoning accuracy by 2.5%-3.2%.

generated critiques in rectifying flawed reasoning steps with 2.5%-3.2% gains in improving reasoning accuracy

evidence_refs.quote: Critic-RM improves reward modeling accuracy by 3.7%-7.3% compared to standard reward models and LLM judges

title: Arithmetic Control of LLMs for Diverse User Preferences: Directional Preference Alignment with Multi-Objective Rewards
problem_statement: Fine-grained control over large language models (LLMs) remains a significant challenge, hindering their adaptability to diverse user needs. While Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) shows promise, its reliance on scalar rewards often limits its ability to capture diverse user preferences in real-world applications.

core_ideas.name: Directional Preference Alignment (DPA)
core_ideas.mechanism: DPA incorporates multi-objective reward modeling to represent diverse preference profiles and models user preferences as directions (unit vectors) in the reward space for user-dependent control. It trains a multi-objective reward model and fine-tunes the LLM with a preference-conditioned variant of Rejection Sampling Finetuning (RSF).

training a multi-objective reward model and then fine-tuning the LLM with a preference-conditioned variant of Rejection Sampling Finetuning (RSF)

metrics: performance trade-off across various reward objectives

title: FLAME: Factuality-Aware Alignment for Large Language Models
problem_statement: The conventional alignment process for large language models fails to enhance factual accuracy and often increases hallucination. This occurs because supervised fine-tuning can train on novel knowledge and reinforcement learning reward functions can prefer longer, more detailed responses that may be less factual.

title: LeDex: Training LLMs to Better Self-Debug and Explain Code
problem_statement: Self-debugging is crucial for LLMs to refine generated code using execution feedback, but prior prompting-based methods perform poorly on small open-sourced LLMs. LeDex proposes a training framework to improve self-debugging by generating explanation and refinement trajectories and filtering them via execution verification.

title: ReFT: Reasoning with Reinforced Fine-Tuning
problem_statement: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotations for Large Language Models (LLMs) shows limited generalization ability because training relies on only one annotated reasoning path per question. title: Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization in Direct Alignment Algorithms
problem_statement: Direct Alignment Algorithms (DAAs) like Direct Preference Optimization avoid separate reward modeling but still suffer from reward over-optimization or hacking, where optimizing the proxy objective degrades true quality. title: RL on Incorrect Synthetic Data Scales the Efficiency of LLM Math Reasoning by Eight-Fold
problem_statement: The paper investigates when training LLMs on model-generated synthetic data helps or hurts math reasoning performance. It finds that while finetuning on synthetic correct problem-solution pairs offers modest gains, training on self-generated data can double efficiency but also amplifies spurious correlations. The core problem is addressing these scaling issues to achieve consistent performance improvements.

title: Mixture-of-Experts Meets Instruction Tuning:A Winning Combination for Large Language Models
problem_statement: The core technical problem is how to improve the performance and efficiency of large language models (LLMs) for task-agnostic learning. The paper investigates combining Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures with instruction tuning to enhance learnability without increasing inference cost. It specifically examines whether MoE models benefit more from instruction tuning than dense models across different experimental setups.

explicit_limitations.name: MoE underperformance without instruction tuning
explicit_limitations.description: In direct finetuning on individual downstream tasks without instruction tuning, MoE models overall underperform dense models of identical computational capacity.

title: PoliTune: Analyzing the Impact of Data Selection and Fine-Tuning on Economic and Political Biases in Large Language Models
problem_statement: The paper addresses the problem of understanding and controlling economic and political biases in Large Language Models (LLMs) when they are fine-tuned for specific ideologies. It investigates how data selection and fine-tuning methods impact these biases, particularly in the context of aligning models with targeted political viewpoints.

explicit_limitations.name: Resource-intensive model for data selection

title: Language Models are Super Mario: Absorbing Abilities from Homologous Models as a Free Lunch
problem_statement: Language models require retraining or GPUs to acquire new capabilities, which is computationally expensive. The paper addresses how to absorb abilities from homologous models without retraining. It focuses on merging multiple task-specific models into a single model with diverse capabilities.

core_ideas.name: DARE (Drop And REscale)
core_ideas.mechanism: DARE sets most delta parameters to zero and rescales the remaining ones to approximate original embeddings, enabling parameter fusion without affecting abilities.
evidence_phrases: set most delta parameters (i.e., the disparity between fine-tuned and pre-trained parameters) to zeros without affecting the abilities
randomly Drops delta parameters with a ratio $p$ And REscales the remaining ones by $1 / (1 - p)$

core_ideas.name: Parameter fusion of homologous models
core_ideas.mechanism: Use DARE as a plug-in to sparsify delta parameters of multiple SFT homologous models and merge them into a single model by parameter fusing.
evidence_phrases: use DARE as a versatile plug-in to sparsify delta parameters of multiple SFT homologous models for mitigating parameter interference and merge them into a single model by parameter fusing

experiment_signals.setting: Encoder- and decoder-based LMs with SFT delta parameters
experiment_signals.claim: DARE can eliminate 90% or even 99% of delta parameters and merge multiple task-specific LMs into one LM with diverse capabilities; in large-scale LMs, the merged LM can surpass the performance of any source LM.
